content: We can frame the working memory retention mechanisms needed for long-horizon active learning as a hierarchical system of specialized biological agents, where each agent selectively retains only information that produces a tangible effect on future learning steps, rather than storing all transient encountered data. Only agents that detect high-impact information relevant to long-term task goals keep their allocated memory chunks active, while low-impact agents voluntarily release their memory capacity to avoid overflow over extended learning horizons.
source_topics: Working memory retention mechanisms for long-horizon active learning
trigger_topic: Working memory retention mechanisms for long-horizon active learning
